Title: Calls grow to strengthen child protection laws after Selayang abuse claims
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Calls are mounting for the government to strengthen key child protection laws following disturbing allegations of abuse at an unregistered care home in Selayang, with legal experts warning that weak enforcement has left vulnerable children exposed.
Former Malaysian Bar president Salim Bashir said urgent amendments are needed to existing legislation, including the Child Act 2001 and the Child Care Centre Act 1984, to ensure stricter enforcement and tougher penalties against operators of unregistered childcare facilities.
He said laws such as the Local Government Act and the Education Act should also be reviewed to mandate regular monitoring and inspections of all childcare centres, as well as to ensure that only licensed facilities are publicly listed.
"The government should take meaningful action by amending and strengthening legislation concerning children, which would provide a stricter approach and harsher penalties in cases involving unregistered care homes," he told the New Straits Times.
Salim said that beyond legal reform, the government must also address structural shortcomings, particularly in ensuring sufficient capacity in state-run homes.
"The government must provide adequate funding to create enough placements in government-run homes to cater for vulnerable children.
"A shortage of places will inevitably push them into unregistered centres, creating a vicious cycle of abuse, maltreatment and operators profiting at the expense of children's welfare and care."
He added that resolving such complex issues requires coordinated efforts between local authorities, the Social Welfare Department and the Education Ministry, alongside support from the wider community.
Salim also called for a comprehensive workforce plan to ensure proper care for children placed in institutions until they reach the age of 18.
Meanwhile, lawyer Nizam Bashir said the operation of an unlicensed care centre for over a decade raises serious questions about enforcement under existing laws.
He said care centres are required to be registered under Section 4 of the Care Centres Act 1993, and that no person should operate or be involved in managing such a centre without proper registration under Section 5.
"As for how it could have operated for over a decade without registration, without in any way condoning what has happened, perhaps there was a slip between the cup and the lip.
"What matters now is that remedial steps are taken immediately and greater oversight is exercised, given that these institutions involve children," he said.
Nizam pointed to gaps in enforcement, saying that the act does not appear to impose a duty on authorities to proactively identify or monitor unregistered care centres.
"As a consequence, enforcement becomes reactive, largely complaint-driven rather than proactive."
He added that questions should also be raised over whether the Social Welfare Department has sufficient resources and whether its operations are adequately automated to facilitate oversight.
"It may be time for a proper committee to undertake a holistic review of the Care Centres Act 1993.
"Nothing matters more than extending the protective arms of the state over children."
On accountability, Nizam said ultimate responsibility lies with the state.
"For a start, the buck stops with the state. Proper accountability means reviewing existing legislation and the agencies responsible for enforcing the law.
"That aside, public awareness is equally important. The public must understand that the Care Centres Act exists and that there are obligations under it.
"We must refuse to look away and step up for the sake of the children."
Four children who previously lived in an unregistered care home in Selayang have come forward with
allegations
of sexual abuse, systematic grooming and violence.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Honda Malaysia vendors back ministry's development-driven policy
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Honda Malaysia Vendors Club (HMVC) has expressed support for the Investment, Trade and Industry Ministry's development-driven investment approach, saying it is crucial for sustaining growth and strengthening the country's automotive ecosystem.
HMVC president Kam Cheong Loong said close collaboration between the government, private sector and multinational companies has long been a key factor in the evolution of Malaysia's automotive industry.
This has enabled balanced policies that support growth while ensuring the sustainability of domestic players.
Kam said such measures have helped deepen participation among Malaysian companies involved in the manufacturing of parts, components and sub-components, contributing to a thriving segment within the sector.
He said the ministry's handling of proposed inward investments, including those involving China-based electric vehicle (EV) maker BYD, reflects a calibrated approach that balances foreign investment with national priorities, particularly in the emerging EV space.
He welcomed the continued development of the automotive sector into EVs and future technologies, but emphasised that policy frameworks must ensure inclusivity.
This is especially so in the procurement of parts and utilisation of local content, for such investments to deliver their full macroeconomic benefits.
Kam said the ministry's recent initiatives demonstrate alignment between public, private and international stakeholders, underscoring a policy approach grounded in prudence and careful deliberation.
At the same time, he called on automakers to continuously review their procurement strategies to strengthen vendor ecosystems and support a resilient supply chain across all segments.
He also voiced support for Honda Malaysia, noting the company's commitment to expanding localisation efforts to drive growth within the domestic vendor network.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Anwar credits federal policies for Johor growth, highlights Forest City revival
Article:
JOHOR BARU: Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim said federal government policies and support had laid the foundation for Johor's strong economic growth.
It was a fact that the federal administration provided full backing to drive the state's economic progress, he said.
Anwar cited the redevelopment of Forest City and the creation of the Johor-Singapore Special Economic Zone (JS-SEZ) as examples of this cooperation, including efforts to secure an understanding with Singapore on the JS-SEZ.
"Johor has indeed recorded good economic growth, but much of it is due to the federal government's vision. We revived Forest City and assisted with the Maharani project, which had been delayed for a long time," he said.
"The decision on the JS-SEZ was made by the federal government, and we paved the way.
"We do not deny that the Johor state government machinery assisted, but these are the facts that we need to explain, he said during the Johor Keadilan convention at Ekoflora today.
Anwar is PKR president.
Forest City, near Johor Baru, faces slow sales and under‑occupation, prompting government efforts to revive the development.
Also present were PKR information chief and Communications Minister Fahmi Fadzil, and Johor PKR state leadership council chairman Datuk Seri Dr Zaliha Mustafa.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: SMKPK Bumbong Lima teachers' dedication leads to excellent SPM 2025 results
Article:
KEPALA BATAS: The unwavering dedication of teachers at Sekolah Menengah Kebangsaan Pendidikan Khas (SMKPK) Bumbong Lima, in nurturing students with special needs, particularly those with autism, has borne fruit, with the school's 2025 Sijil Pelajaran Malaysia (SPM) candidates achieving excellent results.
Going beyond the syllabus, these teachers tailor their approaches to meet each student's individual abilities.
School principal Rosiah Mohamad Ariff said the success reflects the collective efforts of teachers who never gave up in guiding students with special educational needs (MBPK) through learning and examination challenges.
"Teachers here do more than just teach - they act as mentors, motivators and even friends to the students. They understand the emotional and behavioural needs of autistic students, who require different approaches.
"Some spend extra time outside school hours to ensure students truly understand a topic before moving on to the next stage, while also using various techniques to maintain their focus," she told Bernama.
Operating since October 2023, the school, which was officiated by Education Minister Fadhlina Sidek, admits students across all categories of special educational needs (MBPK), including hearing, visual, speech and physical disabilities, learning difficulties, and multiple disabilities.
Of the six SPM 2025 candidates - four with autism and two with learning difficulties - all achieved excellent results.
Rosiah said that the school has 51 teachers, but only eight are trained in special education, with a total student population of 150.
"The approaches used include visual learning, intensive repetition, and individual guidance sessions, which focus on each student's strengths. I am grateful to all the teachers for their strong commitment and patience in caring for all the students here," she said.
Bahasa Melayu teacher, T. Selvam, 55, who has over 30 years of experience in special education, said teaching, particularly students with autism, requires great patience, creativity and continuous commitment, to ensure that every student receives fair and effective educational opportunities.
Acknowledging the various challenges, including managing students' behaviour, he adapts his teaching methods while ensuring that students' focus remains optimal throughout the learning sessions.
Mathematics teacher, Mardhati Mohd Said, 44, said each autistic student is unique and requires a different approach, compared with mainstream students.
"Some students are sensitive to sound, while others have difficulty communicating, so we need to be constantly creative and first understand their emotions. I also conduct additional one-on-one classes to help them better understand," she said.
Meanwhile, housewife Nor Hazanirianty Abdul Razak, 44, said that she and her husband did not expect their autistic son, Muhammad Hazeem Zikry Mohd Hazree, 18, to achieve excellent results of 6A and 1B, given his unpredictable condition, especially during tantrums and meltdowns.
"We are from Besut, Terengganu, and placed our full trust in the teachers here. Besides good facilities, the teachers are highly trained in handling special needs children. Alhamdulillah, not only has he improved academically, but Hazeem is now more independent," she said.
Muhammad Hazeem Zikry said that he is happy studying at SMKPK Bumbong Lima, and plans to further his studies in Teaching English as a Second Language (TESL), due to his strong interest in English.
"I would like to thank all the teachers who patiently taught and encouraged us since I first started studying here. Alhamdulillah, their efforts have enabled us to achieve excellent results in the SPM," he said.
Another parent, Rafiqah Samsudin, 51, said that the teachers' tireless efforts not only improved her son, Muhammad Aqil Aiman Fuzi's academic performance - he scored 6A and 1B - but also built up his self-confidence and social skills.
"They are very patient, committed and never give up in educating my child, despite facing various challenges. While staying at the hostel, he has been very happy being with his friends, so I have no worries," said the single mother.
Parent-Teacher Association (PTA) representative, Khairul Anuar Ariffin, 55, also praised the teachers' strong commitment.
"We truly appreciate their sacrifices, as they give their time and even their own money. Some are willing to conduct extra classes until late at night, while also providing food to encourage students to attend," he said. – BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: OPEC+ debates theoretical oil output hike amid Iran war paralysis, sources say
Article:
MOSCOW/LONDON: OPEC+ may approve an oil output increase on Sunday, four sources from the group said, a rise that will largely exist on paper as its key members are unable to raise production due to the US-Israeli war with Iran.
The war has effectively shut the Strait of Hormuz - the world's most important oil route - since the end of February and cut exports from OPEC+ members Saudi Arabia, the UAE, Kuwait and Iraq, the only countries in the group which were able to significantly raise production even before the conflict began.
Other group members such as Russia are unable to increase output due to Western sanctions and damage to infrastructure inflicted during the war with Ukraine.
Inside the Gulf, damage to infrastructure from missile and drone attacks has also been severe. Several Gulf officials have said it would take months to resume normal operations and reach production targets even if the war stopped and Hormuz reopened immediately.
At its last meeting on March 1, just as the war began to disrupt oil flows, OPEC+ agreed to a modest output boost of 206,000 barrels per day for April.
A month later, the largest oil supply disruption on record is estimated to have removed as many as 12 to 15 million barrels per day or up to 15 per cent of global supply.
Crude prices have soared to a four-year high close to US$120 a barrel. Oil prices could spike above US$150 - an all-time high - if flows via Hormuz remain disrupted into mid-May, JPMorgan said on Thursday.
Sunday's meeting will discuss OPEC+ quotas for May, sources said. An increase will have little immediate impact on supply but would signal readiness to raise output once Hormuz reopens, OPEC+ sources have said. Consultancy Energy Aspects called the increase "academic" as long as disruptions in the strait persist.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Cheri and Rambai help relocate wild elephant, bringing relief to 500 villagers
Article:
GUA MUSANG: Two decoy elephants, Cheri and Rambai, were deployed during an operation at Felda Aring 10 here to help coax a wild male elephant to be moved to a more suitable habitat.
The wild male elephant, nicknamed 'Man 10', was captured by the Department of Wildlife and National Parks (Perhilitan) last Wednesday, bringing relief to about 500 people residing in villages nearby.
Khaidir Ibrahim, 39, said the wild elephant conflict had persisted for nearly two years, causing constant fear among the community.
He said the elephants frequently encroached on the settlement, destroying oil palm saplings and ornamental plants in residents' gardens.
"The sightings have been ongoing for almost two years, but over the last three months, the animals became more active around our homes.
"Alhamdullilah, Perhilitan has finally managed to capture two wild bulls that were roaming Felda Aring 10 and 11.
"While this has eased our worries, we know there are still other wild elephants roaming the plantations nearby," he said when met at Felda Aring today.
Another villager, Ainina Afiqah Mohamad Rotik, 29, said the presence of the elephants not only resulted in the destruction of banana trees and landscaping but also threatened public safety as the animals grew bolder.
She said villagers rarely ventured out at night due to safety concerns.
"Previously, the wild elephant damaged a garage and entered house compounds. It was terrifying because the animal would use the paths right next to our homes.
"We are very relieved and grateful that this elephant was successfully captured and relocated today.
"We thank Perhilitan for their swift action in ensuring the safety of workers and residents here," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police hunt driver over viral wrong-way incident on Jalan Klang Lama
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police are tracking down a driver seen travelling against traffic along Jalan Klang Lama in a video that has gone viral on social media.
Kuala Lumpur Traffic Enforcement and Investigation Department (TEID) chief Assistant Commissioner Mohd Zamzuri Mohd Isa said authorities were alerted to the incident at 11.38am and immediately launched checks.
"The incident is believed to have occurred at about 9.44am on April 5 along Jalan Klang Lama, from the Jalan Klang Lama–Jalan Sepadu intersection to the Jalan Klang Lama–Jalan Kuchai Lama intersection, covering an estimated distance of about 500 metres," he said in a statement.
He added that checks through the complaint system found no police report had been lodged over the incident so far.
Police have since initiated efforts to trace the driver and the vehicle involved for offences under Section 42 of the Road Transport Act 1987.
"Efforts to track down the suspect are ongoing," he said.
Mohd Zamzuri urged those with information to come forward.
Members of the public can contact the police hotline at 03-2115 9999 or visit the nearest police station.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Thousands gather outside US embassy in Jakarta to mourn fallen Indonesian peackeepers
Article:
JAKARTA: Thousands of people gathered outside the United States Embassy in central Jakarta today to express condolences for the three Indonesian military personnel killed while serving under the United Nations Interim Force in Lebanon (UNIFIL).
Participants filled the area stretching towards the National Monument (Monas) near Gambir railway station, with some attendees bringing along children and infants as early as 8.30am local time, according to ANTARA News Agency.
Initiated by the Majelis Ormas Islam (MOI), the gathering themed "Berjuta Doa untuk Syuhuda" (A Million Prayers for the Martyrs) saw participants dressed predominantly in white robes, many carrying Indonesian and Palestinian flags.
A stage approximately two metres high was erected across from the embassy, displaying images of the three fallen soldiers.
The three personnel have been identified as Major Inf (Posthumous) Zulmi Aditya Iskandar, Sergeant Major (Posthumous) M. Nur Ichwan, and Corporal First Class (Posthumous) Farizal Rhomadhon.
Five other TNI personnel were injured in the March 29 and 30 incidents, namely Private First Class Rico Pramudia who sustained serious injuries, while four others, First Lieutenant (Inf) Sulthan Wirdean Maulana, Private First Class Deni Rianto, Private First Class Bayu Prakoso, and Private First Class Arif Kurniawan suffered minor injuries.
On April 3, three more Indonesian peacekeepers were injured following an explosion in El Addaiseh, southern Lebanon.
In addition to prayers for the fallen soldiers, participants voiced support for Palestinian independence, with chants and songs accompanying the demonstration, while Palestinian flags were waved throughout the event.
On Saturday, Foreign Minister Sugiono said France as the penholder for Lebanon at the United Nations Security Council (UNSC) has approved Indonesia's request to convene an emergency meeting on the deadly attack against peacekeepers in Lebanon. – BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Anwar: PKR machinery must bridge government, public gap
Article:
JOHOR BARU: PKR president Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim has called on Johor PKR to strengthen unity at all levels and ensure the party machinery serves the people effectively.
Speaking at the Johor PKR convention today, Anwar emphasised the importance of maintaining a mature and disciplined party that remains committed to its reformist mandate since its inception.
"Johor PKR must continue to strengthen the spirit of togetherness at all levels, regardless of rank.
"We must remain a party that is mature, disciplined and steadfast in upholding the reformist trust we have carried since our founding," he said.
Anwar, who is also the prime minister, highlighted Johor's track record of producing members of parliament entrusted to represent the people, particularly in addressing the rising cost of living and current national issues.
He urged leaders to use their mandate to serve the public by mobilising the party at the grassroots level, rather than pursuing personal interests.
Addressing current challenges, including geopolitical tensions in the Middle East, Anwar called for a concerted effort to communicate the government's Madani initiatives.
These include targeted fuel subsidies, basic supply assurances and ongoing measures to strengthen national economic resilience.
"Priority must be given to ensuring that all policies and initiatives reach the people effectively, with benefits felt on the ground, without deviating from the original objectives," he said.
He also underlined the critical role of the party machinery as a conduit between the government and the public to ensure the dissemination of accurate information.
This, he said, is essential to alleviating public concerns and reinforcing confidence in the nation's direction.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 3 Bangladeshis, Thai national jailed for overstaying and illegal entry
Article:
PASIR MAS: Three Bangladeshis and a Thai national were imprisoned after failing to pay fines for overstaying and illegal entry.
The four, all men, had earlier pleaded guilty to the charges before Sessions Court judge Zulkifli Abdullah.
The three Bangladeshis, aged 25, 28 and 38, were charged under Section 15(1)(c) of the Immigration Act for overstaying.
The Thai national, aged 49, was charged under Section 6(1)(c) of the same Act for illegal entry..
A court official on condition of anonymity told the New Straits Times that the 4 men failed to pay the fine.
"The four did not pay the fines and were sent to the Pasir Mas Satellite Prison," the official said.
All four were detained in an operation by the state Immigration Department on March 29.
The case was prosecuted by Deputy Public Prosecutor Siti Khaidijah Khairuddin.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sabah Umno respects Abdul Rahman Dahlan decision to resign as division chief
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: Sabah Umno respects Datuk Seri Abdul Rahman Dahlan's decision to resign as the party's Tuaran division chief, said state liaison chief Datuk Jafry Ariffin.
"Well, it is his decision and Sabah Umno accepts it with an open heart. We also wish him the best of luck," he said briefly to the New Straits Times.
It was reported that Abdul Rahman, who was the state deputy chief, had announced his resignation as the Tuaran Umno chief.
In a statement yesterday, Abdul Rahman said his resignation was based on personal considerations and not related to any issues within the party.
In an Instagram post, the former Kota Belud member of parliament, who held several ministerial posts in the federal government, said he had the privilege to serve, contribute, learn, and lead.
In the 17th Sabah general election, Barisan Nasional nominated and announced Abdul Rahman's candidacy for the Tempasuk state seat, but he decided to withdraw from the contest.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Farmers urged to switch to organic fertilisers amid El Nino concerns
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: Farmers are encouraged to switch to organic fertilisers to reduce reliance on imported agricultural inputs and lower costs.
Sabah Agriculture, Fisheries and Food Industry Minister Datuk Jamawi Jaafar said the prolonged El Nino weather phenomenon is expected to disrupt fertiliser supply, potentially driving up production costs.
He said a significant portion of the country's fertiliser supply depends on imports, including from China and countries affected by global conflicts.
"When exports of key fertiliser materials such as urea are reduced, prices in the local market will rise.
"I would like to encourage farmers to switch to organic fertilisers as an alternative to reduce costs," he said in a statement.
The Melalap assemblyman said the Sabah Agriculture Department has implemented various training programmes to help farmers produce their own fertiliser using plant and food waste.
"I will continue to engage with farmers on the ground and provide education, including through a special programme with entrepreneurs on April 12 in Karambunai.
"This is important to ensure crop yields remain stable despite extreme weather challenges," he said.
He added that the Sabah Meteorological Department has indicated that the hot and dry conditions caused by the phenomenon are expected to persist until July.
"In these hot weather conditions, I urge the agricultural community, livestock breeders, fishermen and players in the food industry to exercise caution.
"Avoid open burning and refrain from clearing new land for now, as the risk of fires and declining yields is high," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Incentives for carmaker must drive supply chain growth, says PVA
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Incentives for investments in the national automotive industry should create a multiplier effect, driving growth across the entire supply chain and supporting vendor development, said the Proton Vendors Association (PVA).
Hence, the PVA said it fully supported the Ministry of Investment, Trade and Industry (MITI) in its efforts to safeguard and strengthen Malaysia's automotive ecosystem.
"While Malaysia continues to welcome foreign investments, it is important to recognise that automotive companies operating here benefit from various incentives and a well-established industrial base.
"In return, such investment incentives must deliver broader value to the nation beyond immediate commercial gains," the association said in a statement today.
The PVA said incentives channelled to carmakers should also support the development of Tier 1, Tier 2 and Tier 3 vendors.
"This approach ensures long-term sustainability, strengthens local capabilities and reinforces Malaysia's position as a competitive and resilient automotive hub," it added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Muhyiddin: PM should not call critics 'ignorant', must avoid derogatory remarks on energy crisis
Article:
MUAR: Perikatan Nasional (PN) has reminded Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim to maintain decorum and avoid using derogatory labels against those criticising the government's handling of the energy crisis.
PN deputy chairman Tan Sri Muhyiddin Yassin said the prime minister should remain open to feedback instead of labelling critics ignorant following backlash over rising fuel costs linked to the Middle East conflict.
"Use proper language. There is no need to label others with inappropriate words.
"What is being voiced by all parties, including the opposition and the public, is focused on the actual issues and problems being faced right now," he said.
Muhyiddin said that as the prime minister, Anwar should view such opinions as a challenge to improve rather than a personal affront.
"The prime minister should not lash out with such words. He should accept the criticism because it is not just the opposition speaking out; even government supporters are angry.
"We should not call people 'bebal' or anything of the sort. That is poor language.
"In a crisis, the views shared by various parties often come from those who understand the ground reality better.
"The government must be concerned and remain open-minded," he said.
The Pagoh member of parliament was speaking at the Pagoh parliamentary and Bukit Kepong state constituency Hari Raya open house at the Pagoh Sports Complex here.
Also present was Bukit Kepong assemblyman Datuk Dr Sahruddin Jamal.
Muhyiddin added that the transport, agriculture, and industrial sectors were all struggling and required efficient and wise management from the administration.
Yesterday, during an official programme in Ipoh, Perak,
Anwar criticised leaders
across the political spectrum for their remarks on the government's crisis management.
Anwar defended the government's efforts, saying that Malaysia maintains the price of RON95 petrol at a subsidised RM1.99 per litre, which is among the lowest in the world.
He added that the government continues to bear RM6 billion in monthly diesel subsidies for specific sectors.
Anwar also said that the government had reduced the monthly subsidy cap from 300 litres to 200 litres due to the current economic turmoil.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Anwar: Malaysia's diplomatic efforts on Iran, Gaza issues should be given credit
Article:
JOHOR BARU: Credit should be given to the government's efforts to strengthen diplomatic relations and take a firm stance on issues involving Iran and the Gaza conflict, said Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim.
He added that the government's efforts should be recognised rather than disputed by certain parties.
Anwar said the government's firm stance, including its protests against Israeli and United States attacks on Iran, had helped bolster Malaysia's diplomatic relations, allowing Malaysian ships to pass safely through the Strait of Hormuz.
"Do you think it was easy to persuade the Iranian president to let our ship pass through the Strait of Hormuz? I'm not saying the previous government was unfriendly with Iran, but it's not like what we have now. We can call repeatedly and propose in Parliament to oppose the Israeli and US attacks on Iran.
"Look at how many countries in the world have made statements as strong as Malaysia did on the Iran war. So give the government some credit. The opposition refuses to acknowledge it and thinks it was easy," he said at the Johor Keadilan Convention today.
Also present were Communications Minister Datuk Fahmi Fadzil, who is also PKR information chief; PKR vice president Datuk Seri R. Ramanan; and Johor Keadilan State Leadership Council (MPN) chairman Datuk Seri Dr Zaliha Mustafa.
The prime minister said he was disappointed when the opposition seemed not to appreciate his efforts, while often emphasising his meeting with US President Donald Trump, including discussions in the president's official vehicle, "The Beast."
"It's true that I negotiated, but I did not sacrifice the interests of this country. I negotiated in the car, The Beast, with Donald Trump, and I am not apologetic," he said.
Anwar said he had also told Trump that he met with Hamas leaders to highlight the suffering of the Palestinian people, including children killed in continuous attacks and their confiscated land.
"I said all this to President Donald Trump because I come from the spirit of justice and reform. Many leaders talk about great things, but do they dare to face it?" he said.
The prime minister added that he conveyed a similar message to the previous US President, Joe Biden, criticising what he described as the hypocrisy of the West and the ongoing oppression and occupation in Gaza.
He said Malaysia's firm stance reflected the country's policy and principles of reform, earning it recognition as one of the few nations willing to take a strong position on global geopolitical issues.
Meanwhile, he said Johor's strong economic performance was largely the result of federal government support and policies.
"For example, the Forest City project that we revived, the long-delayed Maharani port that received assistance, and the Johor-Singapore Special Economic Zone (JS-SEZ) were fully supported by the federal government," he said.
Anwar said he was actively involved in ensuring the JS-SEZ agreement could be signed, including multiple discussions with the then Prime Minister of Singapore, Lee Hsien Loong, to reach a mutually beneficial understanding.
"Whoever leads the government, I, as Prime Minister and PKR president, will help Johor. We have come a long way. I am not denying the role of the Johor state government and their machinery, but this is a matter of policy and facts," he said.
He added that the facts were verifiable through official records of the past three years.
Johor recorded a total investment of RM110 billion last year, the highest in the country's history. – BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: National Harmony Act needed to bridge communities, says Lee Lam Thye
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: It is time for Malaysia to introduce a National Harmony Act to bridge communities, foster mutual respect and safeguard the unity that has long defined the nation.
Unity advocate Tan Sri Lee Lam Thye said in today's complex and diverse society, harmony cannot be left to chance but must be actively nurtured through clear principles, fair mechanisms and strong institutional support.
"Recent years have shown that misunderstandings, insensitive remarks, racial and religious provocations, and divisive narratives — particularly those amplified on social media — can quickly erode trust among communities.
"These developments underscore the urgent need for a comprehensive legal framework that promotes unity while preventing actions that threaten social cohesion," he said in a statement today.
Lee said the proposed National Harmony Act would serve several key purposes, including providing a clear legal framework to promote respect, tolerance and peaceful coexistence while addressing acts that incite hatred without curbing legitimate expression.
"The Act would prioritise reconciliation and mediation over punishment, resolving disputes rooted in misunderstanding in a constructive manner. It would also allow early intervention to prevent minor issues from escalating into major tensions and encourage responsible conduct among public figures and social media users.
"Additionally, it should safeguard Malaysia's political stability, economic confidence and international reputation as a moderate and inclusive nation," he said.
He said the National Harmony Act should complement existing laws, not replace them, and must be carefully crafted to protect harmony without restricting legitimate democratic expression.
"The objective is not to silence debate, but to prevent hate, provocation and actions that threaten national unity," he said.
Elaborating further, Lee said it is equally important to establish a National Harmony Commission to ensure the law is implemented effectively.
"The commission should be an independent body responsible for promoting inter-ethnic and inter-religious dialogue, mediating sensitive disputes, conducting public education on unity, advising the government on cohesion policies, monitoring tensions, recommending integration improvements, and collaborating with schools, universities, non-governmental organisations and community leaders.
"Malaysia's diversity is not a weakness, but a precious asset that must be protected. The introduction of a National Harmony Act, supported by a National Harmony Commission, would demonstrate our collective commitment to building a society founded on respect, understanding and peaceful coexistence," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: AI boom lifts Frontken outlook: Kenanga Research
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Frontken Corp Bhd should benefit from the ongoing artificial intelligence (AI) upcycle, underpinned by rising semiconductor demand and strong foundry utilisation, Kenanga Research said.
The firm initiated coverage on the engineering service provider with an "Outperform" call and a target price of RM4.70.
Kenanga said Frontken's specialised equipment cleaning expertise and long-standing relationships with leading global foundries position it well to capitalise on the AI-driven capital expenditure cycle.
It noted that major foundries' revenues are projected to grow at a 25 per cent compound annual growth rate through 2027, which is expected to support sustained high utilisation rates.
This, in turn, is likely to drive demand for equipment cleaning services.
The firm projects Frontken's revenue to grow about 15 per cent annually in the financial year 2026 and 2027 (FY26-FY27), supported by elevated utilisation levels, while earnings are forecast to rise 21 per cent and 16 per cent respectively over the same period.
Kenanga Research noted that higher semiconductor equipment cleaning volumes will be driven by increased fab utilisation, particularly at Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co (TSMC), where utilisation is expected to remain high at around 95 per cent, up from 90 per cent previously.
As advanced-node capacity tightens and remains largely secured through 2026, excess demand is beginning to spill over to second-tier foundries, further boosting demand for cleaning services across the ecosystem.
The firm added that the shift towards smaller and more advanced chip nodes is increasing cleaning intensity, as tighter process requirements and higher sensitivity to contamination require more frequent and sophisticated maintenance.
This trend is expected to be margin accretive for Frontken, as demand rises for higher-value services.
Meanwhile, Kenanga Research said Frontken's gross profit margin had expanded from about 45 per cent in FY20 to 47.6 per cent in FY25, and is projected to increase further to 48.2 per cent in FY26 and 49.1 per cent by FY27.
It added that increased repair and maintenance spending by TSMC correlates positively with Frontken's revenue growth, reflecting the company's growing involvement in advanced semiconductor processes.
Kenanga Research said Frontken's key strengths include its positioning in the AI cycle, established track record with leading foundries, and technical capabilities in supporting advanced semiconductor nodes.
Risks to its outlook include a slowdown in the AI-driven cycle, customer concentration, potential loss of market share, and geopolitical disruptions that could affect the supply of critical materials and overall foundry output.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Multi-agency effort under Clean Rice initiative delivers strong harvest results
Article:
BUTTERWORTH: A pioneering government-industry collaboration to revitalise Malaysia's padi sector is showing clear, measurable success, with the Clean Rice initiative emerging as a scalable model to boost yields, restore farmer confidence and strengthen national food security.
The breakthrough was showcased at the First Clean Rice Walkabout in Kampung Permatang Rotan, Penanti here yesterday, launched by Agriculture Department director-general Datuk Nor Sam Alwi.
Driven by ABS Greentech Sdn Bhd in partnership with Humate USA Inc, and supported by the Penang Agriculture Department, the initiative underscores how innovation and coordinated execution can transform a traditionally challenged sector.
At the centre of the success is second-generation farmer Abdul Rahman Din, whose once underperforming plot is now producing harvests of up to four tonnes following the adoption of organic-based inputs and Integrated Pest Management (IPM) practices.
His turnaround reflects a wider shift on the ground — from uncertainty and declining confidence to renewed optimism and productivity among farmers.
Stakeholders said the visible results have begun to spur interest among other farmers, positioning the initiative as a catalyst for broader adoption across padi-growing areas.
A key factor behind the success was the hands-on involvement of Humate USA's technical team, which remained on-site for over four months to ensure proper implementation, continuous monitoring and effective transfer of technology.
At the local level, execution was led by the Integrated Agricultural Development Area (IADA) Penang under Ismail Iberahim, in close coordination with the state Agriculture Department.
In her address, Nor Sam described the initiative as a significant step towards modernising rice cultivation without compromising Good Agricultural Practices (GAP).
She said the project had been designated as an official observation trial for the next three planting seasons, reflecting confidence in its long-term potential.
"This marks the beginning of a new era where stronger collaboration between government agencies, industry players and farmers is critical to ensuring sustainable increases in yield and national food security," she said.
Nor Sam also called on Humate USA to explore establishing a manufacturing hub in Malaysia to lower input costs and strengthen local supply chains, while urging ABS Greentech to expand its IPM-driven model nationwide.
The initiative has drawn support from multiple agencies and stakeholders, including the Malaysian Agricultural Research and Development Institute (Mardi), the Muda Agricultural Development Authority (Mada), Padiberas Nasional Berhad (Bernas), Agrobank and the Northern Corridor Economic Region (NCER).
Industry observers said the programme represents the first successful harvest under a structured intervention combining technology, sustainable practices and multi-agency coordination.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Authorities bust subsidised diesel syndicate, seize over RM320k in goods
Article:
BUTTERWORTH: Authorities have crippled a syndicate involved in the misappropriation of subsidised diesel, seizing nearly RM328,000 worth of goods in a raid here last night.
The operation, conducted under Ops Tiris 3.0 and Ops Jeriji, followed a month-long intelligence-gathering effort.
Penang Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry (KPDN) enforcement officers who moved in on the premises found evidence of ongoing illegal activities involving controlled subsidised goods.
Preliminary investigations revealed that the location was being used as a transfer point to siphon diesel from one tanker lorry to another.
Further checks showed that the premises did not possess any valid licence or permit to store or handle scheduled controlled goods, in breach of existing regulations.
Authorities seized two lorries — a UD and a Hino — two tanker lorries, a set of hoses and a pump, as well as several business-related documents to facilitate investigations.
They also recovered an estimated 21,190 litres of suspected diesel, bringing the total value of the seizure to RM327,963.80.
Two Indian nationals, both in their 20s, were detained at the scene to assist in investigations.
State KPDN director S. Jegan said the modus operandi involved the illegal transfer of subsidised diesel between tanker lorries at an unauthorised storage site.
"This syndicate is believed to have used the premises as a transit point to transfer subsidised diesel without proper documentation or permits, in clear violation of the law," he said at the state KPDN exhibit store here this afternoon.
Jegan said the case is being investigated under Section 21 of the Supply Control Act 1961 for possession of controlled goods with intent to commit an offence.
"We will not compromise with any party found abusing subsidised goods meant for the people. Strict action will be taken against those involved," he said.
He added that KPDN would continue to intensify enforcement efforts to ensure subsidised goods reach their intended recipients and are not diverted for illicit gains.
"This operation reflects our continued commitment under the 'Kita Gempur' initiative to combat leakages and manipulation in the distribution of controlled goods," he said.
If convicted, individuals may face fines of up to RM1 million for a first offence and up to RM3 million for subsequent offences, or imprisonment of up to three years, or both.
Companies may be fined up to RM2 million for a first offence and up to RM5 million for subsequent offences.
Members of the public with information on the misappropriation of controlled or subsidised goods are urged to lodge complaints through KPDN's official channels, including its WhatsApp hotline, e-Aduan portal, call centre, or the EzADU mobile application.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MACC: Compliance alone insufficient to sustain integrity reforms
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia must move beyond strengthening compliance systems and focus on building genuine trust and an ethical culture to sustain integrity reforms.
Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) head of the competency and research branch, Dr Azlan Abd Aziz said while Malaysia has made significant progress in strengthening governance frameworks, reinforcing anti-corruption laws and establishing integrity units, breaches continue to surface, raising concerns about the effectiveness of current approaches.
He said integrity initiatives across organisations are typically built on awareness, enforcement and monitoring, including ethics training, policy enforcement, internal audits and reporting systems aligned with global standards.
"However, the effectiveness of these initiatives is often constrained by challenges in implementation," he said in a statement today.
Azlan said inconsistencies remain a major issue, with programmes frequently affected by limited resources, competing priorities and operational pressures.
"As a result, key initiatives such as training, audits and monitoring may be delayed, scaled down or treated as secondary obligations, creating a structural imbalance between strong frameworks and uneven outcomes," he said.
He added that many organisations continue to treat integrity as a compliance exercise rather than a core value.
"When integrity is operationalised as compliance, ethical behaviour becomes performative. Employees may follow rules without embracing the principles behind them, resulting in compliance-driven integrity, which is inherently fragile," he said.
Azlan also highlighted persistent concerns surrounding whistleblowing mechanisms, despite existing legal provisions and reporting platforms.
"Employees remain hesitant to report misconduct due to fears of retaliation, career repercussions and lack of trust in protection systems. Without confidence in these mechanisms, transparency cannot be fully realised," he said.
He said the MACC's role has expanded beyond enforcement to include preventive measures such as integrity audits, corruption risk assessments and advisory functions aimed at addressing systemic weaknesses before misconduct occurs.
However, he said prevention requires collaboration across institutions.
"Integrity cannot be sustained by enforcement agencies alone. It demands shared responsibility across organisations, leadership structures and workplace culture," he said.
Azlan warned that inconsistent leadership accountability could undermine institutional credibility, especially when enforcement appears selective.
"When integrity becomes negotiable, it loses its authority," he said.
He also pointed to resource constraints as a limiting factor, noting that integrity initiatives are often not backed by sufficient financial and human capital despite being framed as strategic priorities.
"The issue is not a lack of policy, but a lack of integration between policy, practice and culture," he said.
Azlan said discussions on complementary oversight mechanisms, including a potential ombudsman framework, reflect a growing recognition that broader accountability structures are needed.
He said that while the MACC remains a key pillar in Malaysia's integrity ecosystem, its effectiveness depends on how well institutions internalise ethical values.
Among key reforms proposed, he said integrity must be embedded into organisational culture, whistleblower protection must be reinforced through credible action, and leadership accountability must remain uncompromising.
"Integrity must also be recognised as a long-term investment, requiring sustained resources and commitment," he said.
Azlan said Malaysia has laid a strong institutional foundation, but the next phase of reform must focus on translating systems into lived realities.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Keadilan convention: Anwar tells party leaders posts are no guarantee of GE candidacy
Article:
JOHOR BARU: PKR president Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim has reminded division leaders, particularly in Johor, to intensify efforts in building party strength ahead of the next general election.
He said that holding a position as a division, Women, or Youth chief was no guarantee of being selected as a candidate in the next general election.
Anwar added that he would not sign any candidacy memorandums unless the divisions were sufficiently prepared for the polls.
"If you do not perform at the division level, do not make demands. We have plenty of experience with those who sulk, protest, or threaten to quit. We have been through it all. The bedrock of any final decision is the strength of support," he said.
"When the election approaches, the talk is great, the theories are plenty, and the strategies are sophisticated, but the strength on the ground is lacking. Therefore, you are reminded to mobilise the party's strength."
"A division chief is not an automatic candidate, nor is a Women or Youth chief."
"Those who automatically become candidates are the ones who work, are dedicated, and are capable of winning for the party," he said during the Johor Keadilan convention at Ekoflora here today.
Anwar, who is also the Tambun member of parliament, said party members should not become complacent after becoming part of the government.
He told members to demonstrate sincerity and high levels of commitment in building strength at the grassroots level.
"We must remember that it was not easy to reach where we are today; we have gone through various experiences.
"This party is built on struggle. If we want to defend our position, we must lead by example.
"This is because we do not yet have the strength to decide everything. We must prepare ourselves before the election arrives," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Middle East war hits Britain's fish and chip shops
Article:
BEFORE war erupted in the Middle East, fisherman Peter Bruce spent about £5,000 (US$6,600) on diesel to trawl the North Sea for haddock and cod, the main species used in fish and chips.
"Now, the last trip we spent about £10,000," he told AFP, despite his crew reducing the speed of the boat to save fuel.
Bruce, whose boat, Budding Rose, is based in the Scottish port of Peterhead, estimates the extra costs over a year could exceed £100,000.
It was not yet clear whether the rise in energy prices would have an immediate effect on the price of fish, Bruce said.
But he is worried that the hike in fuel prices will hit customer demand for the classic British meal that was already facing challenges on numerous fronts.
Bruce's catch is sold at auction in Peterhead before being transported around Britain and abroad. He fears that customers "will stop buying so much fish and chips and they'll stop going out for meals so much."
The classic recipe for fish and chips – white fish deep-fried in batter, chips, and mushy peas, often with tartare sauce on the side – dates back to the 1860s.
Fish and chip shops usually serve nothing else and most are take-away.
There were around 10,500 fish and chip shops in the UK in 2024 – roughly the same as a decade earlier – according to Seafish, a public body supporting the industry.
This was more than the total number of outlets of the nine main fastfood dining brands – including KFC, McDonald's and Domino's Pizza, it boasted.
Easter is a particularly busy time for the sector since many Britons eat fish on the Good Friday holiday.
Andrew Crook, who owns a fish and chip shop in Lancashire, northwestern England, told AFP the number of customers can more than double on Good Fridays.
Nonetheless, "we're definitely under pressure," said Crook, president of the National Federation of Fish Fryers.
He blamed several factors.
"We've got extremely high fish prices, we've got energy prices; wages go up continually," he lamented.
The war in the Middle East, now in its second month, caused a record monthly rise in petrol and diesel prices in the United Kingdom in March was caused by the conflict, according to data from the Royal Automobile Club (RAC), a British auto services company.
But there are other difficulties.
Stricter fishing rules to conserve species, as well as the war in Ukraine – Russia previously supplied 30 to 40 per cent of the country's fish – had weakened the sector in Britain already, says Crook, and forced it to raise prices.
Another concern is the soaring price of fertilisers and its impact on production of potatoes and oil seeds. Some 30 per cent of the world's fertiliser normally passes through the Strait of Hormuz, which is now almost closed.
Back in 2022, small businesses were worried about a shortage of sunflower oil, since Ukraine was the world's leading exporter of the seeds; now, they are increasingly concerned about the energy cost of heating their cooking oils.
Crook, who sells fish and chips for £11.45 (US$15) per portion, said businesses are looking at options to avoid putting up prices, such as choosing less costly fish species like pollack from North America and South African hake, and reducing portions.
"We do try to cushion consumers," he said, adding that he does not want to raise his prices for now.
* The writer is from AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: PN ready for talks with govt on energy crisis, says Muhyiddin
Article:
MUAR: Perikatan Nasional (PN) is ready to sit down with the Madani government to find solutions as the world grapples with a global energy crisis.
PN deputy chairman Tan Sri Muhyiddin Yassin said the opposition welcomed Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim's call for cooperation but said that the invitation must be sincere.
"The prime minister must be sincere in this effort to resolve issues arising from the Middle East crisis. We welcome such intentions because this is not just an issue for the government, but one that involves the people and the nation," he said.
"However, it cannot be a mere superficial gesture. We are sincere and serious.
"We can sit together and discuss what can be done.
"If the prime minister is open to inviting opposition representatives to sit on a high-level committee, a task force, or any existing platform, we would very much welcome it," Muhyiddin, who had a 17-month tenure as the eighth prime minister, said.
Muhyiddin was speaking to reporters at the Pagoh parliamentary and Bukit Kepong state constituency Hari Raya open house at the Pagoh Sports Complex here.
Also present was Bukit Kepong assemblyman Datuk Dr Sahruddin Jamal.
Yesterday, during an official programme in Ipoh, Perak, Anwar told all parties to work together to find solutions amid the global energy crisis.
He stressed that the current situation was not about political rivalry and that focus should remain on national challenges.
Meanwhile, Muhyiddin voiced his disagreement over the appointment of Tan Sri Mohd Hassan Marican as chairman of the Crisis Management Task Force under the National Economic Action Council.
The task force was formed to coordinate government efforts in handling the energy crisis following the conflict in the Middle East.
The Pagoh member of parliament said the position should instead be held by the prime minister, drawing parallels to his own approach during the Covid-19 pandemic.
"A matter as critical as this should be led by the prime minister himself. This is not just about fuel prices; it is about the grievances of the people who are pressured by the rising price of diesel.
"I do not wish to use the Covid-19 era as an example, but it sits within the same context of a national crisis," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Man arrested over security guard's death in Klang
Article:
SHAH ALAM: Police have arrested a man suspected of killing a security guard with a car steering lock during an altercation in Klang early this morning.
Klang Utara police chief S. Vijaya Rao said the incident occurred about 1am at a security post in a residential area in Bandar Bukit Raja.
He said a dispute broke out between the 40-year-old local suspect and the victim after the latter reprimanded the man, who was drinking with friends during a security patrol.
"The victim sustained injuries from the assault and was rushed to Shah Alam Hospital, where he was pronounced dead at 4.18am," he said in a statement.
Police believe the victim was attacked with a blunt object.
Vijaya said officers from the Klang Utara district police headquarters (IPD) later tracked down and arrested the suspect, and seized a car steering lock believed to have been used in the incident.
Checks showed the suspect has eight prior criminal records and two drug-related offences.
The case is being investigated under Section 302 of the Penal Code.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Terengganu MB calls for stronger Muslim unity amid geopolitical strain at ummah meet
Article:
KUALA NERUS: Muslim unity must be continuously strengthened as a bulwark against present-day challenges, particularly geopolitical strain and an increasingly turbulent world, Terengganu Menteri Besar Ahmad Samsuri Mokhtar said.
He said efforts to unite the ummah must be driven in an integrated manner, encompassing the discourse of knowledge, leadership and economic development.
Samsuri was speaking at the 5th International Congregation for Ummah Unity and the 2026 Malay World Assembly, held at the Sports Complex grounds in Gong Badak here tonight.
He said the programme served as an important platform to bring together thinkers, leaders and scholars from within and outside the country to discuss issues affecting the Muslim community.
"As host, we extend our highest appreciation to all speakers, paper presenters, orators and invited guests from Asean nations and across the country who shared their views at this congregation," he said.
The programme's flagship event was also attended by Pas president Tan Sri Abdul Hadi Awang, Kelantan Menteri Besar Datuk Mohd Nassuruddin Daud, Bersatu vice-president Datuk Dr Mohd Radzi Md Jidin, and Opposition Leader Datuk Seri Hamzah Zainudin.
Other leaders present included Pejuang president Datuk Seri Mukhriz Mahathir, Berjasa president Zamani Ibrahim, Pas secretary-general Datuk Seri Takiyuddin Hassan, Gabungan Nasionalis chairman Aminuddin Yahaya, and Muafakat Nasional president Tan Sri Annuar Musa.
Samsuri, who is also Perikatan Nasional (PN) chairman, said four key issues were discussed during roundtable sessions held throughout the programme.
He said these covered efforts to manage differences in fiqh (Islamic jurisprudence) perspectives and schools of thought in the Malay world as a model for Islamic unity at the regional level.
"Also discussed was the role of Islamic leadership in the Asean region in shaping a new direction for political movements and the character of the ummah.
"The discussions also touched on building Muslim economies in the Nusantara region and efforts to strengthen the identity of the ummah in facing internal and external challenges," he said.
He expressed hope that the outcomes of the discussions could be used to strengthen ummah unity and deliver a positive impact on the development of Muslims in the region.
Earlier, Samsuri joined a motorcycle convoy this morning alongside Kedah Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Muhammad Sanusi Md Nor, Kelantan Menteri Besar Datuk Mohd Nassuruddin Daud, and Perlis Menteri Besar Abu Bakar Hamzah.
He said the convoy, comprising more than 15,000 motorcycles, was symbolic of a journey as one ummah.
"InsyaAllah, the small steps taken today will become a great foundation for a stronger ummah in the future. May our efforts be blessed," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Govt to speed up diesel subsidy reimbursements for petrol station operators
Article:
KOTA BARU: The Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry will engage the Finance Ministry to expedite reimbursement payments to petrol station operators facing cash flow pressures under the targeted diesel subsidy scheme.
Minister Datuk Armizan Mohd Ali said the ministry acknowledged concerns raised by operators, who claimed they were required to front capital for diesel purchases at elevated prices.
He noted that while payment mechanisms are primarily a commercial matter between operators and oil companies, the government is taking the issue seriously.
"We will be holding discussions with the Finance Ministry to work out an appropriate strategy, including enabling reimbursements to be made as quickly as possible," he said.
"We understand that operators must cover upfront purchases, but oil companies are also under strain," he added.
Armizan was speaking at a briefing on Op Tiris 4.0 (Integrated) Enforcement and the Control of Supplies Act 1961 here today.
Media reports yesterday said petrol station operators claimed they had effectively become unofficial financiers of the targeted diesel subsidy distribution due to the upfront payment structure.
Malay Chamber of Commerce Malaysia president Norsyahrin Hamidon was reported as saying the situation was unfair, as operators were effectively financing the subsidy using their own capital.
The chamber said it had received complaints from the Bumiputera Petrol Station Operators Association over the financial burden caused by rising diesel prices.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Body of 63-year-old wheelchair-bound man found 5.5km from where he fell into river
Article:
KUALA SELANGOR: The body of a 63-year-old wheelchair-bound man feared drowned in Sungai Kampung Kuantan was found 5.5km from where he is believed to have fallen into the river.
Selangor Fire and Rescue Department assistant director of operations Ahmad Mukhlis Mukhtar said the victim, identified as Ravi a/l Periasamy, was discovered by members of the public about 8.30am near a jetty at Mile 7½.
"The victim was located at the riverbank approximately 5.5km from the point he was believed to have fallen.
"Firefighters were sent to retrieve the body," he said when contacted today.
He said the department received an emergency call at 9.09pm on April 3. Personnel from the Bestari Jaya Fire and Rescue Station were dispatched to the scene, about 9km away.
Mukhlis said a search and rescue operation was launched immediately using water surface search techniques, with assistance from the Water Rescue Team (PPDA) from the Shah Alam and Port Klang fire stations, as well as the K9 unit.
"On the second day of operations, divers from the PPDA unit conducted underwater searches beginning at 9am, while ground teams combed the riverbanks.
"K9 units were also deployed to assist in searching a one-kilometre radius from the last known location," he said.
Despite extensive efforts, the victim was not found on the second day, and operations were suspended at 6.30pm before resuming the following morning.
Mukhlis said the body was eventually located by the public, bringing the operation to a close.
He added that 13 firefighters, including six from the PPDA unit and three from the K9 unit, were involved, along with three police personnel.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Nik Nazmi to PKR: Do not 'lose your way' at the height of power
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: PKR has been reminded not to "lose its way" and to continue upholding the idealism of the 'reformasi' fight, even at the height of power.
The reminder came from its former vice-president Nik Nazmi Nik Ahmad, who recalled the party's early days when it was driven by hope and idealism as a platform to champion the reform agenda and multiracial politics.
The Setiawangsa member of parliament also questioned whether the party is now more inclined to find excuses to delay change rather than working to implement the reform agenda it has long championed.
"For more than 20 years, the party has fought corruption, upheld justice for all and championed the principles of reform. It has grown stronger, produced representatives from various races and formed state governments.
"Now, PKR is not only a government party, but also a PM (prime minister) party. We need to reflect; when we claim to still champion reform, but the way we handle criticism: is it any different, or is it the same as how Barisan Nasional and Perikatan Nasional handled criticism in the past?
"Are we still attracting those driven by idealism, or only those seeking power. Are we more inclined to find reasons to delay change, or are we striving to implement it to the best of our ability?
"It is easy for people to join a party at the height of its power, when positions and privileges can be offered. When the party is pressured by the government, when party leaders are in and out of lockups, when the only reward is the struggle for idealism, this is when those who are truly sincere come to lend a hand to the party," he said in a Facebook post.
Yesterday marked the 27th anniversary of Parti Keadilan Nasional (PKN), which later merged with Parti Rakyat Malaysia to form PKR.
Nik Nazmi said that at the time, PKN had nothing but hope and idealism as a platform to champion reform and multiracial politics.
"Back then, it was a party born out of hardship on the streets. Unlike other new parties, PKN did not attract ministers or senior government leaders.
"Instead, it was supported by civil society and ordinary people. I was a Form Five student then, and two years later I helped the party, which was seen as a mosquito party with no future," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Structural reforms needed to tackle food security risks
Article:
LETTER:
Global food systems are entering a dangerous phase, driven by the combined pressures of climate change and geopolitical conflict.
At least 1.4 million people are already facing catastrophic levels of hunger, while food crises are worsening across multiple countries due to war, extreme weather and economic shocks.
This is no longer a distant threat. In conflict zones, food systems have collapsed — with production disrupted, supply chains severed and prices soaring. Climate-induced droughts and extreme weather are further weakening global food output.
Malaysia is not immune. Our heavy reliance on food imports exposes us to global volatility.
Supply disruptions, export restrictions and currency pressures are already translating into higher food prices locally.
The burden falls hardest on lower-income households, where rising food costs directly affect nutrition and well-being.
These vulnerabilities are not new. For decades, agriculture has been deprioritised in favour of other sectors, weakening domestic production capacity and deepening import dependence.
At the same time, current development practices are eroding the very resources that sustain our food supply.
Coastal reclamation and unchecked shoreline development are damaging marine ecosystems that provide key sources of protein.
Fish-breeding grounds are being destroyed, coastal catches are declining and small-scale fish operators are increasingly marginalised.
In agriculture, reliance on commercial seeds, chemical inputs and capital-intensive systems has created an imbalanced value chain.
Profits accrue largely to input suppliers, intermediaries and large capital interests, while farmers bear rising costs and shrinking returns. This weakens local production and undermines national food sovereignty.
The result is a fragile food system — externally dependent, structurally unequal and increasingly vulnerable to global shocks.
What is needed is not incremental adjustment, but structural reform. Malaysia must prioritise domestic food production through sustainable, locally grounded approaches that strengthen resilience to climate risks.
Protection of agricultural land, coastal ecosystems and fisheries resources must become a national priority.
Small farmers and fishermen must be supported with fair market access, appropriate technology and policy backing that ensures value is not disproportionately captured by other actors in the supply chain.
Trade policies must also be recalibrated to balance openness with strategic self-reliance.
Food security is not merely an economic issue — it is a matter of national stability and sovereignty.
M.S. ANUAR MAHMOD
Secretary
RURAL Malaysia — Persatuan Pengguna Luar Bandar & Ekologi Malaysia
* The views expressed in this article are the author's own and do not necessarily reflect those of the New Straits Times
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Kayaking incident: MOE to assist families of two trainee teachers who drowned
Article:
KANGAR: The Education Ministry (MOE) is providing immediate assistance to the families of two trainee teachers who drowned during a kayaking activity in Kedah.
In a statement, the ministry said psychosocial support is also being offered, with attention given to the welfare of those affected.
"MOE expresses its deepest condolences to the families of the two Perlis campus Institute of Teacher Education (IPG) students involved in the incident during an off-campus outdoor education programme on Saturday.
"We hope their families are granted strength and resilience in facing this difficult time," it said.
The incident occurred at Pantai Merdeka near Sungai Petani yesterday.
MOE said the case is under investigation by the authorities, while the ministry has also initiated an internal probe to examine compliance with standard operating procedures (SOPs).
"All educational institutions under MOE are reminded to ensure that all outdoor activities strictly adhere to existing SOPs, with participant safety given the highest priority," it said.
The victims were identified, via the official IPG campus Facebook page, as Christopher Ling Jia Siang, 21, and Joey Ling Lin Siang, 20.
Kuala Muda police chief ACP Hanyan Ramlan said both bodies were found at about 7.30pm.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: JPJ audits trailer company after Segamat crash kills three, driver found positive for meth
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 5 — The Road Transport Department (JPJ) has launched a safety audit on the trailer company involved in a crash that killed three people in Segamat, as authorities move to tighten scrutiny on operator compliance.
The audit, carried out under the department’s “JPJ Inspection and Safety Audit” (JISA), will examine whether the company met required safety standards following the fatal collision at Kilometre 212 of the Johor Bahru–Seremban road on April 2.
According to
Buletin TV3
, JPJ director-general Datuk Aedy Fadly Ramli said the review was being conducted in line with instructions from Transport Minister Anthony Loke and at the request of the Land Public Transport Agency (APAD).
“In line with instructions from Transport Minister Anthony Loke, JPJ is conducting a JISA audit on the trailer operator to ensure all safety aspects are adhered to, based on requirements set by APAD for the company.
“The audit team will prepare a report for JPJ so that further action can be taken together with APAD.
“At the same time, JPJ has also suspended the Goods Driving Licence (GDL) of the trailer driver until investigations are completed,” he said.
He said investigations are expected to conclude soon.
Loke had earlier ordered JPJ and APAD to carry out an immediate audit and investigation into the company, stressing that firm action must be taken.
He said the operator must be held accountable for failing to monitor the discipline of its driver, who tested positive for methamphetamine.
The crash, which occurred at about 3.45pm on Thursday at the Taman Golden Hill junction in Gemas Baharu, Segamat, left three family members dead and two others injured after a trailer slammed into the rear of their van.
The trailer driver has been remanded for four days after initial screening found him positive for methamphetamine.

Title: No free passes: Anwar tells PKR leaders to earn GE16 spots through groundwork
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 5 — Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim has warned PKR leaders there will be no automatic tickets to contest in the next general election, urging party divisions — especially in Johor — to prove their strength on the ground or risk being overlooked.
Speaking at the Johor Keadilan Convention 2026 in Johor Bahru, the PKR president said positions within the party — whether division, Women’s or Youth chief — do not guarantee candidacy, according to
The New Straits Times
.
“A division chief is not an automatic candidate, nor is a Women or Youth chief,” he said, stressing that only those who work, show dedication and are capable of winning would be considered.
Anwar added he would not endorse any candidacy memorandums unless divisions demonstrated sufficient preparedness for the polls, making clear that performance — not title — would decide who gets the nod.
“If you do not perform at the division level, do not make demands. We have plenty of experience with those who sulk, protest, or threaten to quit. We have been through it all. The bedrock of any final decision is the strength of support,” he said, as reported by the national daily.
He cautioned that as elections draw near, rhetoric and strategy often outpace real grassroots strength — a gap he said must be urgently addressed.
“When the election approaches, the talk is great, the theories are plenty, and the strategies are sophisticated, but the strength on the ground is lacking. Therefore, you are reminded to mobilise the party’s strength.”
Anwar, who is also the Tambun MP, warned party members against complacency after entering government, urging renewed focus on grassroots work and discipline.
“This party is built on struggle. If we want to defend our position, we must lead by example,” he said, adding that PKR had yet to reach a position where it could “decide everything” without stronger public backing.
“We must prepare ourselves before the election arrives.”

Title: Skip outdoor play: Nancy advises childcare centres to shield kids from heat
Article:
KUCHING, April 5 — Women, Family and Community Development Minister Datuk Seri Nancy Shukri has urged all childcare centres to suspend outdoor activities in hot weather to ensure the well-being of children, particularly those under three.
She said children at childcare centres should remain in sheltered areas and not be exposed to high temperatures, which could pose health risks.
“Operators are reminded to keep children in protected areas. Therefore, they shouldn’t be taken outside to play during hot weather,” she told reporters at the Bank Rakyat Riang Ria Syawal event here today.
Also present was Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Fadillah Yusof.
Nancy also emphasised the important role of parents in keeping their children safe and preventing excessive exposure to the heat.
She said children should be taught about the dangers of hot weather so they can take appropriate precautions.
In addition, she noted that the Social Welfare Department (JKM) is fully prepared, with more than 4,785 personnel deployed nationwide, including 150 counsellors and psychology officers, to support communities affected by the heat. — Bernama

Title: No more stage bus route cancellations, says Perak rep after commuter complaints
Article:
IPOH, April 5 — No more stage bus routes in Perak will be discontinued, including the Kuala Kangsar-Lenggong-Gerik route, following concerns among commuters, Perak Infrastructure, Energy, Water and Public Transport Committee chairman Datuk Mohammad Nizar Jamaluddin said.
“We understand the concerns of users, especially students who depend on this bus service for school. Therefore, the state government will ensure that no routes are discontinued,” he told reporters here today.
The Kuala Kangsar-Lenggong-Gerik route had ceased operations last week but was reinstated two days ago following complaints from users, particularly students who depend on the service for their daily commute.
Mohammad Nizar said that to ensure the continuity of public transport services, the state government is also planning to introduce new routes covering the Kampar and Gopeng areas.
He said the proposed routes would involve Tanjung Tualang, Kampung Timah, Tronoh Mines, Gopeng, Kopisan and Kampar, but are still subject to approval from the Land Public Transport Agency (APAD).
The media previously reported that residents in Lenggong had appealed for the Kuala Kangsar-Lenggong-Gerik stage bus service to be reinstated after it was discontinued from April 1, affecting daily activities of various groups. — Bernama

Title: Bukit Merah at critical stage as Perak keeps close watch on dam levels amid prolonged heat
Article:
IPOH, April 5 — The Perak state government is maintaining close and detailed monitoring of water levels in the state’s main dams, which have been declining due to prolonged hot weather.
State Infrastructure, Energy, Water and Transportation Committee chairman Datuk Seri Mohammad Nizar Jamaluddin said the Bukit Merah dam’s water level stands at 6.965m, continuing to decline and now at Stage One Critical Irrigation status.
He said that the Temengor dam has experienced a continuous decline in water levels since early January, with the current level at 239.07m, approaching the dam’s safe operational critical level of 238m.
“Overall, dams at critical levels require continuous monitoring, disciplined management of water releases, and prudent water usage,” he told a press conference here today.
He added that, generally, water supply throughout the state continues to be stable and under control.
“All water treatment plants are operating efficiently at the required production levels, except for those facing raw water shortages at the intake points,” he said. — Bernama

Title: Wrong way on Old Klang Road: KL police seek white Proton Saga driver after viral video
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 5 — Police are searching for the driver of a Proton Saga after a video showing the car travelling against traffic along Jalan Klang Lama went viral.
Kuala Lumpur Traffic Investigation and Enforcement Department chief Assistant Commissioner Mohd Zamzuri Mohd Isa said police were alerted to the 40-second clip at about 11.38am today.
Initial checks found the incident is believed to have taken place at 9.44am, along a roughly 500m stretch between the Jalan Klang Lama/Jalan Sepadu junction and the Jalan Klang Lama/Jalan Kuchai Lama junction.
“Checks through the complaint system so far show no police report has been lodged in connection with the incident.
“However, Kuala Lumpur JSPT has taken immediate action and is working to trace the driver and the vehicle involved,” he said in a statement today.
He added that the case is being investigated under Section 42 of the Road Transport Act 1987 for reckless and dangerous driving.
Members of the public with information are urged to come forward to assist investigations.
The video, recorded by another road user, shows a white Proton Saga driving against the flow of traffic along the busy stretch.

Title: No polls, no distractions: Umno backs Anwar, says tackle cost-of-living crisis first
Article:
MANJUNG, April 5 — Umno has welcomed Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim’s indication that the 16th general election (GE16) will not be held in the near future, asserting instead that priority must be given to addressing the rising cost of living.
Umno secretary-general Datuk Asyraf Wajdi Dusuki said that, given current global uncertainties, all parties should set aside power struggles and focus on the welfare of the people.
“At a time when the country is facing a major crisis, we should not be overly preoccupied with the pursuit of power and positions.
“What is most important is for the nation’s leaders to remain focused on ensuring the people can cope with the increasingly acute cost of living,” he said.
He told reporters this after officiating the 2026 Concurrent Umno Branch Meetings and the Lumut Umno Division Aidilfitri Open House at Dewan Merdeka, Manjung Municipal Council, here today.
Also present was Umno Supreme Council (MKT) member Datuk Seri Zambry Abd Kadir, who is also the Lumut Umno Division Chief.
Yesterday, Anwar indicated that the Dewan Rakyat would not be dissolved anytime soon to make way for GE16, as the government aims to focus on economic recovery and public well-being.
